title: Self-Sustainable Reconfigurable Intelligent Surface-Assisted mmWave Indoor Communication System
authors: Zhenyu Li, Ozan Alp Topal, Özlem Tuğfe Demir, Emil Björnson, Cicek Cavdar
publish: IEEE Transactions on Wireless Communications
abstract: In the design of a metasurface-assisted system for indoor environments, it is essential to take into account not only the performance gains and coverage extension provided by the metasurface but also the operating costs brought by its reconfigurability, such as powering and cabling. These costs can present challenges, particularly in indoor dense spaces (IDSs). A self-sustainable reconfigurable intelligent surface (ssRIS), which retains reconfigurability unlike a static metasurface (SMS), achieves a lower operating cost than a reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS) by being self-sustainable through power harvesting. In this paper, in order to find a better trade-off between metasurface gain, coverage, and operating cost, the design and performance of an ssRIS-assisted indoor mmWave communication system are investigated. We simplify the use of the ssRIS by considering a preset-based element splitting scheme for maintaining self-sustainability and the formation of coverage groups by associating ssRISs with the closest user equipments (UEs). We propose a two-stage iterative algorithm to maximize the minimum data rate by jointly deciding the association between the UEs and the ssRISs, the phase shifts of the ssRISs, and allocating time resources for each UE. The non-convex optimization problem is tackled using the feasible point pursuit successive convex approximation method. To understand the best scenario for using ssRIS, the resulting performance is compared with that achieved with RIS and SMS. Our numerical results indicate that ssRISs are best utilized in a small environment where self-sustainability is easier to achieve when the budget for operating costs is tight.

title: Viscosity Solutions of Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman Equations for Nonzero-Sum Differential Games with Safety Consideration
authors: Chunhe Ma, Zhi Feng, Shuo Feng, Yipeng Pang, Guoqiang Hu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automatic Control
abstract: In this paper, the solutions of a class of infinite-horizon, nonzero-sum differential games are studied in the viscosity solution sense while considering the system's safety. The value functions, which are solutions of Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman equations, are assumed to be continuous so that the safety constraint defined by the control barrier function can be incorporated. We present a new idea for solving nonzero-sum differential games by approximating their closed-loop solutions via a collection of static noncooperative games. Novel algorithms based on fixed-point iteration and static noncooperative games are proposed to approximate the solutions of nonzero-sum differential games, both with and without the safety constraint. The cost function values, evaluated at the Nash equilibrium points of unconstrained static noncooperative games and at the generalized Nash equilibrium points of constrained static noncooperative games, converge to the solutions of the associated nonzero-sum differential games under the proposed fixed-point iteration scheme. The approximation errors of the proposed algorithms are analyzed. Numerical examples are provided at the end to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed methods.

title: Deep Reinforcement Learning for Intentional Controlled Islanding of Power Systems
authors: Ruoheng Wang, Siqi Bu, Zixuan Zhu, Gaojunjie Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: Intentional controlled islanding (ICI) is often considered as a last resort to avoid power systems from a full blackout induced by large disturbances like cascading failures. This urgent task entails rapid and effective control in a few seconds, making most optimization algorithms intractable in efficiency due to the combinatorial and nonlinear nature of ICI. To overcome the problem, this article proposes a reinforcement learning (RL) method, named RL-Merge, to learn from the past and make real-time decisions, which can efficiently explore for quality solutions while guaranteeing the “N-island” constraint in ICI problems. Concretely, a “merge” strategy is proposed to modify existing solutions by “merging a bus into its opposite island” at a time and guarantee the constraint via a designed processing rule following a merging action. It allows the RL agent to be integrated into any warm-start solver for performance enhancement. An attention-based modeling is designed for the RL agent to structurally handle the features from both problem parameters and objective values of the current solution, leading to notably improved generalizability to unseen scenarios. Comparative case studies with various benchmarks are conducted, validating that RL-Merge is a more capable and efficient solver for both planning and real-time control tasks.

title: Alzheimer’s Disease Classification using Deep Learning Models with SMOTE-based Data Balancing
authors: Aanuj Maheshwari, Swaraj Jahirabadkar, Shrikant Kale, Aditya Game, Anupama Phakatkar
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Inventive Computation Technologies (ICICT)
abstract: Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is a progressive neurological and degenerative disorder that significantly impairs cognitive function, making accurate and early diagnosis essential for effective clinical treatment. Recent advancements in Deep Learning (DL) and Artificial Intelligence (AI) have demonstrated strong potential in automating AD diagnosis using Neuroimaging data, particularly Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI). DL based frameworks hold significant promise for reliable and scalable AD diagnosis in real-world healthcare environments. This survey paper presents a comprehensive analysis of DL architectures, including Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), ResNet50, and InceptionV3, for AI-driven AD classification. The study evaluates model performance on the National Alzheimer’s Coordinating Centre (NACC) dataset, considering a four-class diagnostic framework: Non-Demented, Very Mild Demented, Mild Demented, and Moderate Demented. To address the class imbalance present in the dataset, SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique) is employed to enhance model generalisation and learning fairness. Experimental results indicate that InceptionV3 demonstrates superior performance as compared to baseline CNN and ResNet50. This survey also discusses key future directions for research involving explainable AI, multimodal learning, and personalised diagnostic systems.

title: Deep Learning–Driven Classification of Brain Tumors from MRI Scans Using Convolutional Networks
authors: R. Umanesan, Utkarsh Roy, Shiksha Bharti, S. Jagadeesh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Futuristic Technology
abstract: Accurately detecting brain tumors from MRI scans is important for early diagnosis and treatment planning. Human-based manual analyses are time-consuming and prone to human mistakes. Herein, a CNN has been developed which automatically classifies MRI data into brain tumor and non-brain tumor classes. In this work, a balanced MRI dataset of tumor positive and healthy patients is gathered and preprocessed using standard techniques, including resizing, normalization, data augmentation using random flipping, rotation, zooming, and adding noise. A CNN is proposed comprising several convolution layers (32, 64, and 128 filters) followed by ReLU activation functions, max-pooling, regularization using dropout, and FC layers terminated with a sigmoid output layer. The network is trained from end-to-end by the Adam optimizer. When the model is tested, it gives approximately 96% classification accuracy on the test set, as reported. These results can be viewed as indicating that even a relatively simple CNN can perform well in differentiating tumor versus non-tumor MRI scans. These findings substantiate the hypothesis that CNN-based image classifiers will have excellent potential in the clinical domain for automatic and reliable brain tumor screening.

title: Adaptive Cross-Domain Representation Learning for Neural Player Piano Sheet Transcription under Noisy Conditions
authors: Xueying Wang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Inventive Computation Technologies (ICICT)
abstract: Achieving high-precision piano score transcription in complex noisy environments remains a significant challenge. The core challenges lie in the distributional differences and structural heterogeneity between acoustic signals and standard notational spectra, as well as the semantic fluctuations caused by noise interference. To address these challenges, this paper proposes an Adaptive Cross-Domain Representation Learning (ACDRL) framework for noisy environments. This framework is used to achieve robust mapping from noisy acoustic input to standard piano score notation. It constructs a joint musical embedding space, unifying the modeling of pitch, duration, harmony, and temporal context. Simultaneously, a noise-aware conditional mapping mechanism is introduced as an optimization. This mechanism explicitly injects noise distribution information during the embedding generation process. At the structural modeling level, this model designs a dynamic heterogeneous graph representation method for music. Simultaneously, at the algorithmic level, a self-supervised mask encoding and reconstruction mechanism is proposed. This mechanism can improve the model's representation consistency and structure preservation capabilities in weakly supervised and unpaired scenarios. In the decoding stage, the concept of continuous dynamical systems is introduced into the neural piano score transcription task for the first time. Furthermore, a continuous evolutionary decoding mechanism based on the Runge–Beta evolutionary equation is constructed, which achieves continuous updating of semantic states through fourth-order Runge–Kutta numerical integration. Experiments are conducted on a self-developed cross-domain dataset containing 4,260 high-quality notated musical scores and 3,980 real and synthetic noise recordings. Results show that the proposed method outperforms comparable methods in Note-Level Accuracy, Onset F1, Offset MAE, Pitch Deviation, Harmony Structure Consistency, and Noise Robustness Index. Compared to the suboptimal model GraphMusicTranscriber (GMT), the Offset error is reduced by approximately 10.7%, and the pitch deviation is reduced by approximately 16.1%. Even under extremely low signal-to-noise ratio conditions (-4 dB), the model accuracy remains above 65%. In completely unpaired cross-domain training scenarios, the model's transcription accuracy remains above 80%.

title: Re-Reward: A Self-Improving Generative Model Via Reinforcement Learning from Real World Feedback
authors: Sai Kiran Naik Banoth, Sai Deepak Gugulothu, Ravi Guguloth, Javali Subha Mapati
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Inventive Computation Technologies (ICICT)
abstract: Even while long-context large language models (LLMs) have advanced significantly, the supervised fine-tuning (SFT) model's long-context performance is frequently impacted by the poor quality of the LLM-synthesized data, which results in inherent restrictions. Additionally, LLMs may act in ways that are detrimental and inconsistent with human morals. The RL stage does not entail direct comparisons, even if the reward is learned via comparing various replies. The instability of reinforcement learning (RL) is made worse by this discrepancy between the stages of RL and reward learning. We address this by putting forth a novel framework, ReReward, an RL-based technique that rewards long-context based model answers from six human-valued variables using an off-the-shelf LLM as judge. By aligning human feedback in real time, Pairwise Proximal Policy Optimization (PPPO), which learns to improve from direct comparison, mitigates the shortcomings of long-context SFT models.

title: Performance Evaluation of Fischer−Huber Bitloading Algorithm for LiFi Over PLC
authors: Atiyeh Pouralizadeh, Dennis Goodson, Volker Jungnickel, Luis Miguel Giraldo, Joaquin Perez Soler
publish: 2026 IEEE 22nd International Conference on Factory Communication Systems (WFCS)
abstract: Integrating optical wireless communication (OWC) with powerline enables a cost-effective solution to extend wired industrial networks to the wireless domain. In this paper, we experimentally compare the performance of both uniform and adaptive bitloading for an 1 m optical wireless link with and without powerline communication (PLC) as fixed backbone. In particular, we evaluate the margin-adaptive bitloading algorithm from Fischer and Huber for light-fidelity (LiFi) over PLC. Unlike rate-adaptive algorithms that maximize the throughput, Fischer and Huber algorithm maximizes the signal to noise ratio (SNR) margin (i.e., minimize error probability) for a fixed target rate, making it ideal for industrial applications where reliability is critical. Using a realistic indoor power grid and a line-of-sight (LoS) LiFi link, we analyze the cascaded channel frequency response (CFR), SNR, bit and power allocation and evaluate the resulting bit error rate (BER) and throughput. Experimental results demonstrate that the Fischer and Huber algorithm effectively mitigates the impact of deep spectral notches introduced by the power grid, enabling reliable and robust transmission at data rates of up to 468 Mbit/s.

title: Distributed Nash Equilibrium Seeking Algorithms With Fixed-Time Convergence for Constrained Non-Cooperative Games on Digraphs
authors: Binghao Ouyang, Xingxing Ju, Yong Wang, Gang Feng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Control of Network Systems
abstract: This paper investigates the problem of distributed Nash equilibrium seeking with fixed-time convergence for constrained non-cooperative games over directed communication graphs. Distributed Nash equilibrium seeking algorithm is proposed based on a leader-following consensus protocol and the projection operator. It is proved that the proposed distributed algorithm converges to the Nash equilibrium in fixed-time, which is independent of the initial conditions of the players. Moreover, the upper bound of the convergence time for the proposed Nash equilibrium seeking algorithm is explicitly given. The effectiveness of the proposed distributed algorithm is validated through an example of a constrained energy consumption game.

title: Zero-Shot Prompting for SMS Spam Detection using Large Language Models
authors: Budi Prasetiyo, Hadid Ramadhan, M. Faris Al Hakim
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Inventive Computation Technologies (ICICT)
abstract: Short Message Service (SMS) spam remains a persistent security threat due to its high delivery reliability, low operational cost, and widespread use as a communication medium. Most existing SMS spam detection systems rely on supervised machine learning or deep learning models that require large labeled datasets and extensive training, which limits their adaptability to rapidly evolving spam patterns. This study investigates the feasibility of applying zero-shot prompting with large language models (LLMs) for SMS spam detection without task-specific training. The classification task is reformulated as an instruction-guided text generation problem, enabling LLMs to perform spam detection through natural language prompts. A comparative evaluation is conducted across four decoder-only LLMs such as Mistral Small 24B, Gemma 2 9B, Qwen 2.5 7B, and LLaMA 3.1 8B using a unified prompt and deterministic inference configuration. Experiments on a balanced subset of the SMS Spam Collection Dataset demonstrate that all evaluated models achieve competitive spam detection performance, with Mistral Small 24B obtaining the highest accuracy of 95%. Further analysis reveals differences in error distribution, model confidence, and inference efficiency across models, highlighting a practical trade-off between classification robustness and computational cost. These findings indicate that zero-shot LLM-based spam detection provides a flexible and scalable alternative to conventional supervised approaches, particularly in dynamic or data-scarce environments.

title: Spatio-Temporal Diffusion Modeling for Cloud Motion Prediction Using INSAT-3DR Multi- Spectral Imagery
authors: Sagar Rajebhosale, Tushar Surwade, Shruti Doiphode, Snehal Akolkar, Himanshu Goyal, Aniket Shirsath
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Futuristic Technology
abstract: The reliable prediction of cloud motion in the short-term is a critical tool for weather nowcasting, aviation safety, disaster response, and solar energy utilization. Various tools to estimate cloud motion have employed traditional approaches, such as optical flow and the use of Cloud Motion Vectors (CMVs), often struggling to accurately model nonlinear and rapidly evolving cloud patterns in a dynamically changing environment. Given the increasingly available high-temporal resolution multi-spectral data from geostationary satellites, there is a strong need to develop models that learn and represent complex spatiotemporal processes of cloud evolution.This study proposes a diffusion-based spatio-temporal generative framework for short-term cloud-motion forecasting using INSAT-3DR/3DS satellite imagery. The model unifies the learnt motion dynamics of prior satellite sequences with the stochastic generative capabilities of a diffusion model to produce the next continuous frames of clouds. The spectral diffusion model produced future representations of cloud deformation, growth, and propagation processes more effectively than traditional deterministic prediction approaches. In the experiments presented, the spectral diffusion model improved continuity of cloud formations, represented more consistent cloud structures, and produced a more visually realistic multi-frame cloud forecasts, showing the potential of using a diffusion-driven model for operational meteorological nowcasting applications.

title: Development of an IoT-Based Real-Time Weather Station for Environmental Monitoring
authors: Arnav Kulkarni, Rohan Raju, Atharva Deshpande, Pravin Gawande, Shailesh Kulkarni
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Futuristic Technology
abstract: The present paper proposes a design and implementation of an Internet of Things (IoT)-based weather monitoring system of real-time data acquisition and analysis based on Raspberry Pi. The mooted system incorporates various environmental sensors like DHT22 which measures the temperature and humidity, BMP180 which measures the atmospheric pressure, and a rain sensor which measures the precipitation. The Raspberry Pi 4 Model B can be used as the main processing unit that will gather sensor data at fixed intervals and send it to a cloud platform where it will be stored and visualized. Experiments prove that the system has a high degree of accuracy with temperature error ranging between -0.5 to + 0.5 °C and humidity error ranging between -2 to +2 %RH when compared to the conventional meteorological devices. The suggested system can provide a cost-efficient localized weather surveillance that can be applied in agriculture, smart cities, and environmental investigations. The overall cost of the system is cheaper than commercial weather stations but with similar accuracy.

title: Splitting Precoding with Subspace Selection and Quantized Refinement for Massive MIMO
authors: Yasaman Khorsandmanesh, Emil Björnson, Joakim Jaldén
publish: IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
abstract: Limited fronthaul capacity is a practical bottleneck in massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) 5G architectures, where a base station (BS) consists of an advanced antenna system (AAS) connected to a baseband unit (BBU). Conventional downlink designs perform all precoding at the BBU and transmit a high-dimensional precoding matrix over the fronthaul, resulting in significant quantization loss and signaling overhead. This letter proposes a splitting precoding architecture that separates the design between the AAS and BBU. The AAS performs local subspace selection, after which the BBU computes a quantization-aware refinement precoding over the resulting reduced-dimensional effective channel. Numerical results show that the proposed splitting precoding strategy achieves higher sum rate than conventional one-stage precoding.

title: Energy-Efficient Parallel Ising SoftMax Engine for Transformer Attention Based on Asynchronous SOT-P-Bits
authors: Yu Liu, Bolin Zhang, Jialiang Yin, Wentao Huang, Zhenyu Guan, Weisheng Zhao, Deming Zhang, Lang Zeng
publish: IEEE Electron Device Letters
abstract: Transformer models have achieved remarkable progress in both natural language processing and computer vision; however, the SoftMax operation has become a major bottleneck in Transformer inference due to costly exponential operations and repeated memory access. We present a hardware-native SoftMax engine based on an asynchronous probabilistic Ising machine built from experimentally fabricated SOT-MTJ P-Bit. A SoftMax-to-Ising compiler maps arbitrary logits into an implementable energy function, enabling direct physical sampling of the corresponding Boltzmann distribution. The resulting Ising SoftMax converges rapidly, exceeding FP16 precision at 32μs. When integrated into neural network workloads, it preserves high inference accuracy without noticeable degradation. These results demonstrate a fully asynchronous physical SoftMax engine and highlight its potential for energy-efficient deep learning acceleration.

title: Spectral Sparsity: A Unifying Framework for Scalable Model Interpretability Using Codes
authors: Justin S. Kang, Darin Tsui, Yigit Efe Erginbas, Landon Butler, Amirali Aghazadeh, Kannan Ramchandran
publish: IEEE BITS the Information Theory Magazine
abstract: As machine learning models grow in complexity and are increasingly deployed in high-stakes applications, the need for tools to interpret their outputs is essential for ensuring their trustworthiness. Existing interpretability methods often fail to scale to modern models because of their high computational cost, or are too simplistic and fail to capture higher-order interactions. This tutorial presents a unifying framework that addresses these challenges, leveraging coding theory to exploit the natural spectral sparsity many models exhibit under the Fourier and Möbius transforms. Algorithms derived from algebraic and sparse-graph codes can be used to efficiently recover this sparse support, revealing important feature interactions across a variety of tasks and modalities. We showcase this framework's utility on applications ranging from uncovering complex linguistic patterns in large language models to identifying epistatic interactions in biological sequence models.

title: A Systematic LLM-Assisted Approach for Deriving Skills from Competencies: A Case in Business Process Management Education
authors: Guilherme Rego Rockembach, María Encarnación Sosa Sánchez, Lucineia Heloisa Thom
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Competency frameworks play a central role in aligning educational objectives with professional practice. However, systematically deriving granular, assessable skills from high-level competencies remains an unresolved methodological challenge across domains. Despite the maturity of several fields, many lack structured and reproducible processes for translating competencies into measurable skills. This article introduces a systematic and generalizable method for deriving skills from competencies using Large Language Models (LLMs) guided by explicit pedagogical heuristics. The proposed pipeline was designed to ensure syntactic compliance, cognitive granularity, traceability to source materials, and controlled generation. Its primary empirical validation was conducted in the domain of Business Process Management, a mature field that nevertheless lacks a consolidated and assessable skill framework. The methodwas first evaluated through an internal quality analysis and expert-based validation of the generated skills, demonstrating consistent pedagogical alignment and model convergence. It was then compared against seven baseline approaches, ranging from classical text processing techniques to direct and fine-tuned LLMs. Results indicate that the heuristic-guided pipeline mitigates common limitations such as hallucination, rigidity, and loss of cognitive control, outperforming fine-tuned models in data-scarce educational contexts. Finally, to assess cross-domain generalizability, the method was applied to Environmental Education and compared against official Brazilian curriculum standards. Semantic similarity scores (0.39–0.82) showed strong alignment for broad cognitive competencies, but revealed coverage limitations for specific disciplinary skills due to corpus constraints. These findings suggest that the proposed approach provides a robust and scalable solution for competency-to-skill engineering in educational contexts.

title: Resilient Smart Grid Operation Through Cybersecurity-Aware Critical Node Protection and Intelligent Risk Control Mechanisms
authors: Rui Hao
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Inventive Computation Technologies (ICICT)
abstract: To address the "physical-information" dual-domain coupled disturbance problem faced by smart grids under the background of high-proportion renewable energy integration, this paper proposes a resilient operation framework that integrates network security awareness, critical node protection, and intelligent risk control mechanisms. This method constructs a four-layer coupled model: physical layer, information layer, decision layer, and control layer. At the topology level, this model introduces a critical node load reconfiguration function to suppress cascading fault propagation. At the risk modeling level, a KAN (Kolmogorov–Arnold Network) nonlinear risk prediction model is constructed based on Kolmogorov–Arnold representation theory. Furthermore, the prediction results are embedded into the rolling optimization process of Model Predictive Control (MPC). Additionally, this study establishes a battery degradation cost function considering temperature coupling effects and incorporates it into a multi-cycle optimization model. The comprehensive experiment was conducted using simulations on an improved 39-node test system. Specific parameters included a total installed capacity of 915 MW, a renewable energy penetration rate of 42.6%, and consideration of multiple uncertainties such as network attack intensity (0–0.9), wind speed (22–46 m/s), and energy storage temperature (18°C–46°C). The proposed method was compared with traditional optimal power flow scheduling (M1), N-1 constrained optimization (M2), a model considering energy storage degradation (M3), an enhanced wind-solar uncertainty model (M4), and the MPC method without KAN prediction (M5). Results show that the proposed method improves the overall resilience index by 6.1%–9.8% in multi-disturbance coupled scenarios. Simultaneously, the expected uncharged energy (EENS) is significantly reduced, and the average system recovery time is shortened by approximately 28%–41%. In a 72-hour rolling optimization experiment, the energy storage degradation cost decreased by 22.2%. Regarding risk prediction performance, the mean absolute error (MAE) of the KAN model was 0.041. This result outperforms ANN (0.084) and LSTM (0.069). The coefficient of determination R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> reaches 0.978, which is higher than that of ANN (0.91) and LSTM (0.94). Furthermore, it can identify rising risk trends in advance during high-risk phases (attack intensity > 0.7).

title: Operand-Dependent Collision Leakage: A Refined Assessment Framework for Modular Multiplication in BRIP Implementations
authors: Haoyuan Li, Hua Chen, Weiqiong Cao, Jian Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: Side-channel attacks pose a critical threat to cryptographic devices, with horizontal attacks being a primary strategy when the secret information is changed in each execution. The modular multiplication is a frequent target of horizontal attacks, which can pose a threat to the secret such as BRIP algorithm. In our research, we found that existing leakage assessment frameworks often perform poorly for modular multiplications in a low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) environment. Our analysis reveals that the leakage magnitude and position are greatly influenced by shared operands, specifically the Hamming distance between corresponding segments processed by the multiplier units. This dependency fundamentally limits traditional approaches. To address this challenge, this paper introduces a novel collision leakage model centered at the multiplier-unit level. The operand-dependent model demonstrates the impact of shared operands on leakage in modular multiplication. Building upon this model, we propose a refined leakage assessment framework that integrates clustering algorithms, the TVLA methodology, and adaptive threshold setting. This framework possesses trace filtering capabilities, dynamically identifying traces with sufficient leakage. To demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework, we design and conduct a horizontal clustering attack on BRIP algorithm in an ECDSA implementations. The results show that traces passing our framework’s filter achieve a high secret scalar recovery success rate. Furthermore, comparative analysis confirms that our framework offers significant advantages over traditional methods like PCA, covariance, and semi-parametric approaches, particularly in low-SNR condition.

title: Attention-Driven Deep Learning Framework for Diabetic Retinopathy Detection Using CBAM and Preprocessing Filters
authors: Akshita M B, Abhinay Kumar, S. Janardhana Rao
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Futuristic Technology
abstract: Diabetic retinopathy is one of the most common causes of visual impairment throughout the world making accurate and fast detection very important and valued in the world today. In this paper, we present a novel deep learning model which serves as a experimental proof-of-concept incorporating Residual Networks (ResNet-101) and Convolutional Block Attention Modules (CBAM) to assist with representation. In order to improve performance, we have also added an edge-aware preprocessing step to the model that combines Canny edge detection and Gaussian blur. The Gaussian filter will attenuate noise, while Canny edge detection has been implemented to maximize the detection of important retinal structures. All data was handled cautiously, including utilizing significant data augmentation to help the model generalize. The model was trained and tested on a [14] Kaggle "Diabetic Retinopathy Detection" dataset, achieving a training accuracy of 95% after 25 epochs, demonstrating improved training behavior compared to base ResNet-based foundations. We find the attention mechanisms combined with edge-aware preprocessing allow the model to be more sensitive to lesser seen pathological changes, creating a firm, experimental framework.

title: A Self-Sovereign and Privacy-Preserving Approach to Decentralized Academic Credential Verification using Zero-Knowledge Proofs
authors: V. Hemamalini, Likhith Kumar Reddy Ponnapati, Aviv P Joji, Garv S Rathore
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Inventive Computation Technologies (ICICT)
abstract: The rapid proliferation of digital educational credentials has intensified challenges related to credential fraud, privacy infringement, and reliance on centralized verification infrastructures. Conventional credential verification mechanisms depend on Public Key Infrastructure managed by centralized Certificate Authorities, resulting in single points of failure, limited scalability, and increased operational overhead. Although blockchain-based credentialing approaches introduce immutability and tamper resistance, such solutions often suffer from a privacy–transparency trade-off, as verification commonly requires exposing complete credential data or associated metadata on public ledgers. A privacy-preserving decentralized framework for Self-Sovereign Identity and Verifiable Credentials is presented to address these limitations. The proposed architecture combines distributed ledger-based trust anchoring with zero-knowledge cryptographic techniques to enable secure and confidential credential verification. Credential commitments are immutably anchored on a high-throughput consensus network, while verification is performed using Zero-Knowledge Succinct Non-Interactive Arguments of Knowledge, enabling proof of credential validity without revealing underlying secrets or sensitive attributes. Verification is conducted without continuous involvement of the issuing authority, thereby eliminating centralized dependency and improving system availability. Experimental evaluation demonstrates that the framework supports sub-second proof generation and millisecond-level verification, while maintaining minimal on-chain storage and predictable operational costs. The results indicate that the proposed approach provides a scalable, efficient, and privacy-respecting solution for decentralized academic credential verification in modern digital identity ecosystems.

title: Self-Aligned Enhancement-Mode IGO FETs for Long-Retention 2T0C DRAM With Suppressed Capacitive Coupling
authors: Jiye Li, Mengran Liu, Hao Ding, Shengjie Yang, Lin Bao, Lei Lu, Shengdong Zhang, Zongwei Wang, Yimao Cai
publish: IEEE Electron Device Letters
abstract: In this work, we demonstrate self-aligned top-gate (SATG) indium gallium oxide (IGO) field-effect transistors (FETs) fabricated by atomic layer deposition (ALD) for capacitor-less (2T0C) DRAM applications. The devices exhibit enhancement-mode (E-mode) operation with a high field-effect mobility of 77.5 cm2/V·s and excellent bias stability. Owing to the minimized parasitic capacitance in the SATG structure and post-deposition annealing (PDA) enabled E-mode IGO channel, the 2T0C cells exhibit a small coupling-induced voltage drop (ΔVSN < 0.06 V) and a long retention over 104 s at 0 V hold. These results demonstrate the strong potential of SATG IGO FETs for BEOL-compatible 2T0C DRAM applications.

title: Monolithically-Integrated Inkjet-Printed Magneto-Electric Dipole Antennas and Arrays
authors: Kevin Martin, Dimitra Psychogiou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: This article reports on a new class of fully monolithically-integrated 3D antennas and antenna arrays based on single-polarized and dual-polarized magneto-electric (ME) dipole elements. A new multi-material piezo drop-on-demand inkjet printing integration scheme is used to facilitate monolithic 3D integration, size compactness and performance enhancement. For proof-of-concept validation purposes, prototypes of single-and dual-polarized antenna elements and arrays were designed, manufactured, and tested at Ku-band. They include: i) a single-polarized magneto-electric (SPME) dipole antenna with center frequency (fcen) of 13 GHz, gain of 6.3 dB and bandwidth (BW) of 4.2 GHz, ii) a dual-polarized ME (DPME) dipole antenna with fcen of 15 GHz, gain of 5.8 dB and overlapped BW of 3.5 GHz, iii) a 1×4 SPME-based antenna array with fcen of 13 GHz, gain of 7.5 dB and BW of 4.61 GHz, and iv) a 2×2 DPME-based antenna array with fcen of 15 GHz, gain of 8.36 dB and overlapped BW of 3.85 GHz.

title: A UB-ETDA Based Spaceborne SAR Parameter Design Method for Enhanced Extended Target Detection Performance
authors: Huaping Xu, Chonghang Wang, Jingwen Li, Bing Sun, Wei Liu, Xianghua Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: For extended target detection, most existing space-borne SAR parameter design methods primarily focus on ensuring normal system operation, with limited consideration of the impact of SAR parameters on extended target detection performance. To enhance the extended target detection performance of spaceborne SAR from a parameter design perspective, a novel parameter design method based on an upper bound on extended target detection accuracy (UB-ETDA) is proposed, which comprehensively accounts for key factors affecting extended target detection performance, including aspect-dependent scattering characteristics of the extended target and spatial correlation properties of clutter. By employing UB-ETDA as a new performance metric to guide spaceborne SAR parameter design, the extended target detection performance of the system can be effectively improved. First, a statistical signal model for spaceborne SAR extended target detection is developed, systematically characterizing the stochastic properties of the extended target, background clutter and noise components in SAR images. Then, the expression of UB-ETDA for SAR system is rigorously derived, along with a stable and statistically efficient numerical computation method. Finally, with UB-ETDA as the primary performance metric and existing performance metrics as constraints, a spaceborne SAR parameter design method is proposed for improving extended target detection performance. Simulation results validate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: Classifying ADHD Presentations Using Temporally Segmented Behavioral Data from a Serious Game
authors: Seung-Jae Kim, Jun-Su Kim, Unhui Jo, Yoo Joo Jeong, Su Jin Jun, Jin-Yeop Park, Hyang-Sook Hoe, Jeong-Heon Song
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Distinguishing attention deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) presentations, such as predominantly inattentive (ADHD-I) and hyperactive/impulsive (ADHD-HI), is clinically important for management. However, traditional ADHD assessments often rely on subjective evaluations, potentially introducing biases and limiting diagnostic accuracy. As a more objective approach, this study aimed to develop a machine learning-based classification model using behavioral data derived from serious games performed by children diagnosed with ADHD. A total of 51 children aged 6 to 13 participated, and both parent-reported Korean-ADHD Rating Scale scores and serious game performance data were collected. Behavioral data collected from serious games were temporally segmented into early and late phases of gameplay, and discriminative behavioral features were identified through a machine learning-based feature selection process. Three machine learning models, Random Forest, Support Vector Machine, and eXtreme Gradient Boosting, were trained and evaluated. Models using temporally segmented behavioral features showed strong classification performance, particularly for the ensemble-based models. Among them, the Random Forest model demonstrated the best test performance, achieving an accuracy of 81.818%, an F1-score of 85.714%, and an AUROC of 83.333%. Furthermore, behavioral differences between the ADHD-I and ADHD-HI groups became more pronounced during the latter half of gameplay. In particular, children with ADHD-I showed longer decision times, longer times to the final touch, more unnecessary touches, and lower maximum difficulty levels than those with ADHD-HI, indicating that cognitive processing becomes slower and response strategies become less efficient as gameplay progresses into the later phase. These findings indicate that models using serious game-derived behavioral data, particularly with temporal feature engineering, can effectively classify ADHD presentations and may serve as a scalable, quantitative tool to support clinical assessment and the development of personalized interventions.

title: Large Language Models in Software Security Analysis
authors: Dylan Wolff, Martin Mirchev, Abhik Roychoudhury
publish: Communications of the ACM
abstract: None

title: AI Driven Precision in Breast Cancer Screening: Transforming Early Detection
authors: Pratiksha Gaddamwar, Ayushi Vinod Telrandhe, Trisha Paul, Suhani Gawate, Sanchita Barade, Yash Pardhi
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Inventive Computation Technologies (ICICT)
abstract: Breast cancer is very important for healthcare providers globally, with good outcomes being achieved through prompt diagnosis. There are currently many ways to use artificial intelligence (AI) to aid in the diagnosis and screening of breast cancer. In this paper, we will look at how AI has been used to benefit the diagnosis of breast cancer by improving population screening methods, helping to determine breast tissue density and providing better interpretation of clinical trial data. In addition to these findings, we have highlighted three key benefits of using AI: the improved accuracy of diagnosing breast cancer, the reduced workload of radiologists and greater access to high-quality diagnostic care. Despite the many benefits that AI brings to our ability to diagnose breast cancer, there are still several barriers to the implementation of AI in screening and diagnostic processes, including limited data and generalization, bias and a lack of explainability. Moving forward, researchers should focus on developing multimodal models for breast cancer diagnosis using AI, developing an explainable and privacy-preserving digital solution and intelligent clinical support systems for breast cancer diagnosis. Overall, the conclusion of this paper is that while the use of AI in the diagnosis of breast cancer is on the rise, it is necessary to first establish and apply the tools responsibly to gain the maximum advantages from this approach.

title: Simultaneous Dual-Parameter Measurement Based on a Modified Vernier Effect at the Equal-FSR Critical Condition
authors: Yunqing Guan, Matthias Fabian, Dongsheng Wu, Yingbin Feng, Hongwei Gao, Tong Sun, Kenneth T. V. Grattan
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: This work both investigates the critical state of the Vernier effect scheme in which the interferometric spectra of the sensing interferometer and the reference interferometer attain equal free spectral ranges (FSRs) at a specific wavelength, and then explores how the zero point of the FSR-difference curve can be used to determine the unique wavelength (UWL) of the Vernier envelope. The analysis carried out has revealed a new mechanism for realizing dual-parameter measurement. Furthermore, building on this, a compact sensing scheme has then been proposed as a proof of the concept, where this mechanism has been verified using only a single sensing interferometer. By selecting the central wavelength of the measurement range as the turning point and generating an inverted spectrum of the sensing spectrum as the reference, the resulting output Vernier envelope features a UWL. This positive approach introduces a uniquely identifiable wavelength in the conventional Vernier envelope and enables dual-parameter sensing by simultaneously tracking the UWL intensity variation and the wavelength shift of the peak or dip adjacent to the UWL. The effectiveness of the proposed scheme has then been experimentally validated through temperature and humidity measurements, as exemplars. The investigation of the critical FSR-equality condition thus provides new insights for increasing the number of measurable parameters, thereby offering a promising pathway towards novel Vernier schemes with distinctive spectral characteristics and enhanced capabilities for multi-parameter measurement.

title: Hybrid ML-PID Control for Dynamic Load Management in Power Electronic Systems
authors: D.Sasikala, Kanimozhi S, K. Sindhuja, Geetha D, Marpu Ramesh, C Srinivasan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Futuristic Technology
abstract: Power electronic systems are more necessitated to function in quickly varying and unexpected load situations. Traditional control systems, such fixed-gain PID, frequently prove inadequate in these situations because of their restricted flexibility. This work introduces a high-performance machine learning (ML) control technique for dynamic load management, with Support Vector Regression (SVR) as the primary prediction model. The SVR algorithm is trained on real-time datasets of voltage, current, and load to precisely predict forthcoming load variations. These predictions are utilized to proactively modify the control parameters of a power converter system, facilitating accurate modulation and enhanced system responsiveness. Simulation findings indicate a substantial improvement in control performance settling time fell by more than 45%, overshoot reduced from 22% to 5%, and steady-state error was practically eradicated. The ML controller attained a 9% increase in total system efficiency while sustaining exceptional stability under all evaluated load conditions. The results validate that the proposed SVR-based methodology provides enhanced adaptability, expedited dynamic response, and strong control precision, establishing it as a high-performance, intelligent solution for power electronic systems, including renewable energy interfaces and smart grid applications.

title: Securing Legacy Systems: Analyzing Historical US Cyberattacks and How WAFs Defend Against Modern Threats
authors: Sai Teja Kotagiri
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Futuristic Technology
abstract: A research study investigates how Web Application Firewalls (WAFs) mitigate legacy system vulnerabilities through an in-depth analysis of major United States cyberattacks, focusing on the SolarWinds supply chain attack, Microsoft Exchange breaches, and the Equifax data breach. It shows how outdated communication protocols, weak encryption, and lack of vendor support in legacy systems create opportunities for attackers to disrupt operations and steal critical information. The study demonstrates that WAFs provide vital protection by offering virtual patching, active traffic inspection, and advanced threat management at the application layer, shielding legacy systems without extensive code changes. Evidence from reported cases indicates that WAF-based defenses can prevent a large share of breach attempts and significantly reduce operational downtime, producing substantial cost savings. Next-generation WAFs additionally use AI and machine learning to detect zero-day attacks and automatically tune security policies. The paper recommends deploying WAFs within layered cybersecurity architectures to protect legacy platforms and support compliance with regulations such as HIPAA and PCI DSS. It also identifies open gaps, including secure cloud migration strategies for legacy systems and integrated approaches that combine WAFs with identity management in hybrid frameworks, suggesting these areas for future research. Finally, the study outlines practical steps for WAF deployment, defining security policies, integrating WAFs with SIEM platforms, and strengthening monitoring, providing actionable guidance for both researchers and practitioners seeking to safeguard legacy infrastructure from complex threats.

title: A Responsible AI Validation Framework for Generative Chatbots Handling PII/PHI
authors: Gayathri Surianarayanan, Srikanth Singireddy, Abinaya Mettupatti Sivagnanam
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Futuristic Technology
abstract: Generative AI chatbot use is growing at a rapid pace in healthcare, payer systems and government benefit programs, however it can only be safely deployed after thorough validation, particularly when these systems process sensitive Personal Identifiable Information (PII) and Protected Health Information (PHI). This paper presents a complete Responsible AI Validation Framework for Generative Chatbots Handling PII/PHI (RAIV-GC) as a multi-layered architecture consisting of privacy leakage testing, fairness and bias assessments, adversarial robustness assessments, factual reliability checks, and regulatory compliance validation. Synthetic healthcare datasets, adversarial prompt simulations, persona-based fairness assessments and multi-turn evaluations are used to demonstrate that RAIV-GC can provide measurable reductions in privacy leakage (84–100%), improvements in adversarial resistance (approximately 80%), reductions in conversational bias (70–75%) and gains in factual accuracy (8–12%) compared to baseline models. RAIV-GC provides healthcare organizations, payers and regulatory bodies with a repeatable, audit-ready method for evaluating the safety of generative AI models before deployment. Additionally, the framework aligns with HIPAA, GDPR, and the NIST AI Risk Management guidelines, addressing critical challenges in the safe development and deployment of AI models in sensitive healthcare environments.

title: Molecular Type Permutation Absorption Modulation: Block-Transmission, Detection, and Optimal Location
authors: Zhanjun Hao, Yiting Zou, Yuankun Tang, Qianqian Wang, Lei Zhang, Weidong Gao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Molecular, Biological, and Multi-Scale Communications
abstract: Unlike most existing modulation schemes for molecular communications, which transmit information through the properties of message molecules, molecular absorption shift keying is a newly proposed scheme that not only conveys information but also provides energy for molecule harvesting nodes. However, the principles of molecular absorption shift keying are tailored for scenarios with a single molecule harvesting node, limiting its versatility. In this paper, we introduce a novel modulation technique called molecular type permutation absorption modulation (MTPAM), where the transmitter encodes the information into the permutation of molecular types of the message molecules and the passive/absorbing states of molecule harvesting nodes. To enhance reliability and transmission rates while reducing the global synchronization requirements among harvesting nodes, we develop a flexible block-based transmission mechanism. Additionally, we implement a majority voting system on each passive link to improve performance. We design both an ideal maximum likelihood detector and a conventional maximum likelihood detector for MTPAM systems. The upper bounds for the bit error rate (BER) of these two detectors are derived. To effectively balance the performance of active and passive links, we utilize Bayesian optimization to quickly identify the optimal locations of harvesting nodes. Simulation results show that our scheme significantly outperforms the existing modulation schemes in terms of BER.

title: TSNN: A Non-parametric and Interpretable Framework for Traffic Time Series Forecasting
authors: Bowen Liu, Haijian Lai, Chan-Tong Lam, Junhao Dong, Benjamin Ng, Wei Ke, Sio-Kei Im
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: Although many complex models were proposed to analyze time series data, some studies have demonstrated remarkable performance with simpler structures. A recent study proposed a non-parametric framework for 3D point cloud classification, which has the potential to be adapted for time series forecasting and enable interpretability. Inspired by the previous works, we present TSNN, a non-parametric and interpretable framework for traffic time series forecasting. TSNN consists of multiple layers that decouple the time series by matching the entries in a memory bank, where the memory bank is constructed using a similar matching process within the training set. It leverages the periodicity in traffic data to enhance forecasting accuracy while maintaining a simple model architecture. The proposed model operates without trainable parameters, preserving its inherent interpretability. In the experiments, TSNN achieves competitive performance compared to the typical deep learning models in four real-world traffic flow datasets. We also visualize the decoupling process to show the effectiveness of the components. Finally, we demonstrate the interpretability of the model and illustrate the contribution of each time step within the memory bank. Our code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/pzzzzzm/TSNN_release</uri>.

title: IGFUL: An Integration Gap-Filling, Unmixing and Learning Approach for Estimating Continuous High-Resolution Landsat Normalized Difference Vegetation Index Time Series
authors: Guodong Zhang, Jiangchuan Hu, Gaofei Yin, Rui Chen, Changjing Wang, Meilian Wang, Yi Zhang, Zongyan Li, Yimin Ni, Gang Sun, Yufang Zhang, Anxin Ding, Xinyan Liu, Aleixandre Verger
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: The Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) is the most popular index used in remote sensing for monitoring vegetation dynamics. While numerous moderate-resolution NDVI products are available, there is an increasing demand for fine-resolution, temporally continuous NDVI time series, particularly for monitoring vegetation dynamics in areas with high surface heterogeneity. A major challenge in producing such datasets is the irregular Landsat data acquisition, often hampered by extensive cloud contamination. This study introduces an Integration Gap-Filling, Unmixing and Learning (IGFUL) approach for creating continuous 30-m, 8-day NDVI time series by integrating Landsat surface reflectance with 250-m GLASS NDVI data. The workflow comprises four stages: (1) constructing the first Random Forest (RF) model using cloud-free Landsat surface reflectance as input and GLASS NDVI as output to obtain physically consistent Landsat clear-sky NDVI; (2) for cloudy pixels on a target day, identifying similar pixels based on seasonal similarity curves and utilizing their spatio-temporal correlation information for pre-filling missing Landsat NDVI values; (3) identifying reference images using Structural Similarity Index (SSIM), calculating temporal and spatial predictions through unmixing and Thin Plate Spline (TPS) methods respectively, and developing the second RF model using temporal prediction, spatial prediction, GLASS NDVI on the target day, and Landsat NDVI and GLASS NDVI on the base day as inputs, with Landsat NDVI on the target day as output; (4) weighting multiple initial predictions from the second RF model to generate the final Landsat NDVI reconstruction results. IGFUL was evaluated under varying cloud conditions and outperformed three benchmark methods in both accuracy and efficiency. It achieved high accuracy at the CIA site (RMSE = 0.013, CC = 0.983) and remained robust at the cloud-heavy Qionglai site (RMSE = 0.033, CC = 0.882). IGFUL's ability to reduce reconstruction errors while preserving phenological and spatiotemporal consistency underscores its potential for generating high-resolution and temporally continuous NDVI time series in challenging remote sensing environments.

title: Optimizing Terrain Shapes for Generating Desired Water Flows
authors: Yuki Kimura, Syuhei Sato, Yoshinori Dobashi
publish: 2026 IIEEJ International Conference on Image Electronics and Visual Computing (IEVC)
abstract: We present a system for optimizing terrain shapes that produce desired water flows such as rivers and waterfalls. The user specifies a target trajectory along which the water should flow, simulated with the Smoothed Particle Hydrodynamics (SPH) method. Our system optimizes the terrain shape to minimize the difference between the simulated particle trajectories and the target trajectory. This optimization is carried out iteratively: terrain normals are updated with a PID-based feedback controller, and the terrain shape is reconstructed by solving a Poisson equation.

title: Meta-Memory for Large Language Models
authors: Bin Liang, Cai Ke, Runcong Zhao, Qinglin Zhu, Lin Gui, Yue Yu, Hui Wang, Ruifeng Xu, Kam-Fai Wong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Audio, Speech and Language Processing
abstract: Integrating memory components into large language models (LLMs) can improve the generation quality for long-term conversations. However, existing memory management methods largely overlook the cognition and regulation of the memory process, lacking the capability to dynamically manage and utilize memory on demand. To address this challenge, this paper approaches Meta-Memory for Memory Management (M$^{4}$), a novel paradigm that equips LLMs with the ability for self-monitoring and self-reflective memory management. In long-term conversations, where dialogue history accumulates continuously, the meta-memory capability of M$^{4}$ enables LLMs to autonomously 1) identify what knowledge needs to be memorized; 2) determine how to construct and store memory; 3) monitor the correctness and validity of the acquired information; and 4) decide when to learn more and how to retrieve information to refine their responses. Experimental results on two long-term conversation datasets and two long-term question-answering datasets demonstrate that our M$^{4}$ significantly enhances the memory management capacity of LLMs in long-term information learning, achieving more efficient storage and higher-quality response generation.

title: Cloud-Enabled Preprocessing and Feature Extraction Framework for Hydrocephalus Detection from MRI Scans
authors: R.B.Maria Sofia, R.Parameswari
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Inventive Computation Technologies (ICICT)
abstract: Hydrocephalus is a brain disorder characterized by an abnormal accumulation of the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) in the fluid-filled spaces of the brain (ventricles), which may cause increased pressure on the brain, mental impediment, and life-threatening complications if not properly treated. Traditional diagnostic practices are based on manual interpretation of MRI scans, which is both time consuming, subjective and resource consuming - particularly inaccessible in rural or under-resourced regions of the world. Conventional diagnostic workflows lack the elements of real-time and are standardized, preventing early diagnosis and intervention. There is a crucial need for a cloud-integrated and standardized preprocessing system for automating the feature extraction process for classification following this step. In this work the technical principles of Phase 1 of a more comprehensive cloud-based framework for hydrocephalus detection are posted and the secure data acquisition and preprocessing and feature extraction from MRI scans are addressed. A dataset containing 1,000 MRI images (500 hydrocephalus and 500 healthy) will be curated from images with different demography. Preprocessing Malaysia included image cropping, normalized audio intensity and its segmentation to pick apart ventricular regions. Quantitative features including ventricle volume, shape morphology and the distribution pattern of CSF will be extracted using advanced medical imaging libraries. The preprocessed data is stored and managed by a scalable cloud infrastructure, so that it is available in real time and protects the privacy of the data in order to provide access to machine learning modules. The processed dataset ensures structural consistency and quality of data, which is a good input for classification models. Integration with cloud storage makes it more accessible and it aids in the development of diagnostic tools in a fast manner. This preprocessing step is essential to create the platform to efficiently perform hydrocephalus detection using a large amount of physiological parameters in real-time and with a low latency.

title: X-Ray Radiation in 126-kV Vacuum Interrupters During Sequential Power–Frequency Conditioning and Comparative Analysis of Conditioning Methods
authors: Wei Sun, Huantong Shi, Xingwen Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Plasma Science
abstract: The pursuit of high-voltage, ecofriendly vacuum circuit breakers (VCBs) as alternatives to SF<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\mathbf {6}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>-insulated apparatus brings to the fore the challenge of X-ray radiation generated during the conditioning of vacuum interrupters (VIs). This article presents an experimental and simulation study on the X-ray emission characteristics of a 126-kV VI. The temporal evolution of radiation during sequential power–frequency voltage conditioning cycles is investigated, and the effectiveness of three conditioning methods is comparatively evaluated from a radiation perspective. A self-designed absorption spectrometer was employed to measure the X-ray dose and spectrum. Results from five consecutive conditioning experiments revealed a progressive reduction in the total X-ray dose alongside significant spectral hardening, indicating a saturation of the conditioning effect after 4–5 cycles. Comparative analysis demonstrates that, while laser preconditioning has a minimal impact (~5% dose reduction), high-current preconditioning substantially reduces the subsequent power–frequency conditioning dose rate by approximately 35% compared to the conventional method. Crucially, high-current conditioning also results in a softer X-ray spectrum, characterized by a lower proportion of high-energy photons, which implies reduced penetration capability and a lower radiation threat. These findings provide insights for optimizing conditioning protocols, enhancing personnel safety, and guiding the design of radiation shielding for high-voltage VI testing and manufacturing.

title: HMO−Hierarchical Monitoring Framework for Heterogeneous Multi-Domain TSN Orchestration in Industry 4.0
authors: Seyedali Hadian, Manuel Schappacher, Dominik Welte, Kedar Naik, Axel Sikora
publish: 2026 IEEE 22nd International Conference on Factory Communication Systems (WFCS)
abstract: Industry 4.0 deployments increasingly rely on deterministic communication across heterogeneous Multi-Domain Time Sensitive Networks. However, current management models provide limited observability and cross-domain monitoring. This work-in-progress introduces the Hierarchical Monitoring Orchestrator, an architectural extension to the fully centralized IEEE 802.1Qcc that enables cross-domain telemetry without interfering with the operational state of individual network domains. We propose a standardized YANG model and a twophase signaling procedure to enable real-time metric correlation for guaranteed end-to-end Quality of Service.

title: A Rotation-Controlled Anisotropic Hourglass Metasurface for Multiband Reflective Linear-to-Circular and Cross-Polarization Conversion
authors: Muhammad Nasir, Slawomir Koziel, Anna Pietrenko-Dabrowska
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This paper presents and experimentally validates a multifunctional anisotropic metasurface (MS) that exhibits linear-to-linear (LTL) reflective cross-polarization (RCP) and circular polarization conversion (CPC) responses across multiple frequency ranges spanning S, C, X, and Ku bands. The proposed MS integrates a single layer designs consisting of 45° oriented truncated circular ring and an hourglass-like structure, providing four LTL-RCP conversion bands at (2.41 – 2.48, 3.16 – 3.46, 5.80 – 6.59, and 11.64 – 13.75) GHz, with polarization conversion ratio (PCR) exceeding 95%, and seven CPC bands at (2.33 – 2.38, 2.55 – 2.99, 3.69 – 5.37, 7.13 – 10.92, 13.95 – 14.25, 14.93 – 15.17, and 15.31 – 15.56) GHz with the axial ratio (AR) less than 3 dB. By only adjusting the angle of the hourglass structure at 15° within the same structure, LTL-RCP with PCR greater than 95% is realized in the frequency bands from 13.71 to 14.64 GHz, along with ≤ 3 dB of AR is achieved at four additional CPC bands covering (2.20 – 2.36, 2.95 – 8.41, 10.84 – 12.30, and 14.84 – 15.06) GHz. The polarization conversion mechanisms are explained through detailed surface-current distributions analysis and full-wave electromagnetic (EM) simulations. Furthermore, the proposed concept is validated through the measurements of the fabricated prototype consisting of 8 × 13-unit cells. The measurement results are in excellent agreement with the simulation predictions. The proposed MS design demonstrates a simple yet effective solution for achieving tunable multi-band polarization manipulation without structural modification, making it suitable for advanced EM communication applications.

title: Dynamic Rendering Control Method in Distributed Rendering for Multiple Users in Shared Virtual Space
authors: Asahi Yamada, Suguru Saito
publish: 2026 IIEEJ International Conference on Image Electronics and Visual Computing (IEVC)
abstract: In multi-user virtual space applications, rendering on user devices becomes difficult when there is a large number of avatars with rich detail and a wide variety of motion. This paper proposes a new server process for a server-side imagebased distributed rendering system designed to deliver rendered results to users. In the proposed method, images of an avatar are shared among multiple users who view the avatar from similar directions, thereby reducing the number of times the avatar needs to be rendered. Specifically, the system dynamically controls the number, positions, and rendering frequencies of cameras on the basis of system load. We report the results of performance measurements and experiments for the system implemented on the TSUBAME 4.0 supercomputer.

title: Teleoperating Mobile Robots Via Vda 5050 - a First Middleware Evaluation Within Open Industrial Networks
authors: Niklas A. Wagner, Lars Toenning, Dennis Lünsch, Jana Jost, Christian Wietfeld, Peter Detzner
publish: 2026 IEEE 22nd International Conference on Factory Communication Systems (WFCS)
abstract: Teleoperation is a practical fallback for mobile robots in intralogistics when rare but safety- and throughputcritical edge cases exceed autonomous capabilities. While Fleet Management Systems (FMSs) increasingly rely on vendor-neutral interfaces such as VDA 5050 to coordinate heterogeneous robots, teleoperation is not natively represented in the standard and is typically implemented as an ad hoc, robot-specific add-on. This paper closes this gap by proposing a VDA 5050 teleoperation extension that enables human-in-the-loop control as part of the same interface used for autonomous task and route coordination. To facilitate reproducible evaluation, we further contribute a configurable VDA 5050 traffic generator that reproduces realistic message patterns for middleware-based FMS communication. Employing the Open Radio Access Network (Open RAN) concept, cellular 5G and future 6G networks can be flexibly deployed in industrial environments to provide reliable, low-latency wireless connectivity for mobile robot teleoperation. Using an end-to-end stack consisting of VDA 5050 messaging, middleware transport, and Open RAN wireless link, we evaluate latency and communication overhead to characterize the performance implications of standardized teleoperation integration.

title: A Multi-Technology Experimentation Platform for Heterogeneous Industrial IoT Communications: From Lpwan to Private 5G Towards 6G-IoT Testbeds
authors: Fabian Sowieja, Robin Thomas, Jubin Sebastian E
publish: 2026 IEEE 22nd International Conference on Factory Communication Systems (WFCS)
abstract: Industrial Internet of Things (IoT) environments increasingly rely on heterogeneous wireless communication technologies to support diverse applications ranging from largescale sensing to reliable industrial connectivity. However, experimenting with and evaluating these technologies within a unified framework remains challenging due to fragmented infrastructures and technology configuration parameters. This demonstration presents a unified multi-technology IoT experimentation platform that integrates LPWAN and cellular communication technologies including MIOTY, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, Wi-Fi, and Private 5G, combined with edge gateways and cloud-based monitoring tools. The platform, developed using IICT GmbH's existing multi IoT communication technology demonstrator infrastructure and extended within the vision of the 6G-IoT Experimentation Platform (6G-IoTEP) project, enables real-time deployment, monitoring, and comparative analysis of heterogeneous wireless communication systems. The demonstration illustrates wireless communication in industrial scenarios where sensor nodes using different communication technologies transmit industrial data to a unified experimentation dashboard, allowing participants to observe performance characteristics such as latency, and reliability. The platform provides a flexible environment for hands-on experimentation, research validation, and education, supporting the development and evaluation of future heterogeneous communication architectures for industrial IoT and next-generation 6G-enabled systems.

title: From Private O-RAN to Automated Spectrum Sharing: Use Case Taxonomy and Scenarios for Spectrum Policy-Driven Control
authors: Arturas Medeisis, Jui-Tang RT Wang, Karolis Kiela
publish: 2026 Open Conference of Electrical, Electronic and Information Sciences (eStream)
abstract: Private 5G Open Radio Access Networks (O-RAN) increasingly rely on heterogeneous access (5G and non-5G devices) and locally coordinated spectrum, yet practical deployments remain constrained by core-anchored traffic paths and limited mechanisms for integrating spectrum sharing policies. This paper links O-RAN architecture with future spectrum access automation concepts by developing an abstract framework for spectrum policy-driven O-RAN control. First, we introduce a taxonomy of private O-RAN use cases defined by mobility, traffic locality, and spectrum constraints. Second, we propose two experimental O-RAN scenarios based on local breakout and heterogeneous gateway integration, enabling evaluation of latency and traffic locality improvements. Third, we define automation-ready hooks that translate spectrum governance signals into actionable Non-Real-Time and Near-Real Time control policies. The proposed framework illustrates how private O-RAN networks can be made to serve as a natural future-proof environment for testing and implementation of automated spectrum sharing concepts.

title: Deep Learning-Based for Accurate Estimation of Hypertension Risk Using Health Data
authors: S.Uma Maheswari, Nadimpilli.Aneela, S. M. Srilanga Meenatshi, L.N.Jayanthi, Monisha R, Gowtham Kumar A S
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Futuristic Technology
abstract: Hypertension is a common cardiovascular disorder that presents significant health hazards, including heart disease and stroke. Precise forecasting of hypertension risk is essential for timely intervention and efficient management. This paper introduces a deep learning methodology employing a Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) to assess hypertension risk based on demographic and health-related information. The model is trained on the Hypertension Risk Dataset from Kaggle, comprising 13 characteristics and a hypertension risk label. The proposed RNN model has robust prediction performance, with an overall accuracy of 95.6%. The result outcomes the efficacy of RNNs in identifying complex patterns and temporal relationships in health data, surpassing conventional machine learning methods. The results indicate that deep learning models can function as dependable instruments for early risk evaluation and individualized monitoring of hypertension. This method can mitigate difficulties, enhance patient outcomes, and facilitate improved healthcare decision-making through the implementation of timely preventative actions.

title: Dual Active Bridge Converter for Electric Vehicle Type–C Charging System
authors: Shrinidhi Chimmalagi, Sameer Nayak, A B Raju, Vinod S Patil
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Futuristic Technology
abstract: Among several topologies, the DAB converter shows promise for high-efficiency bidirectional power transfer in EV charging applications. This paper deals with the design and simulation of a DAB isolated bidirectional DC-DC converter for an Electric Vehicle Type-C charging system using different phase-shift control techniques. The converter is modeled in PLECS and analyzed for Single Phase Shift, Extended Phase Shift, Dual Phase Shift, and Triple Phase Shift schemes in order to evaluate voltage, current, and efficiency performance. The comparative study shows that EPS has significantly enhanced the ZVS range, reduced circulating current, and lowered RMS current stress compared to SPS. This study underscores the importance of op-timized phase-shift modulation towards achieving soft-switching and minimizing conduction losses. Additionally, a closed-loop EPS control scheme using a PI controller has been implemented for output voltage regulation to attain stable bidirectional energy flow. Simulation results confirm that the DAB converter demonstrates high efficiency with reliable operation, making it suitable for future EV Type-C fast-charging systems.

title: Enhancing Multi-Step Solar Irradiance Forecasting through TVF-EMD-AWNN: a Time-Varying Hybrid Framework for PV Power Plant_supp1-3688713.pdf
authors: Sumanta Pasari
publish: N/A
abstract: Accurate solar irradiance prediction is inevitable for estimating photovoltaic energy generation and meeting utility demands. This paper presents a novel TVF-EMD-AWNN hybrid framework that integrates time-varying filter empirical mode decomposition (TVF-EMD) with an adaptive wavelet neural network (AWNN) for enhanced prediction accuracy at an hourly timescale. The proposed framework encompasses a sophisticated three-fold practically relevant integration architecture. First, the adaptive filtering mechanism of TVF enables dynamic frequency time optimization while maintaining non-stationary signal characteristics through its time-varying filtering process. Then, the EMD decomposes these enhanced signals into intrinsic mode functions, exhibiting unique temporal-spectral signatures and facilitating comprehensive multi-resolution analysis. Finally, the adaptive wavelet parameters in AWNN synergistically represent these decomposed components through dynamic scale-translation optimization in the wavelet space. Additionally, the proposed TVF-EMD-AWNN model is also validated through the expanding window cross-validation approach. The theory is illustrated on a long hourly solar irradiance data (2015–2021) at four diverse climatic regions of India. Results demonstrate noteworthy accuracy improvement in terms of relative RMSE reduction in comparison to established standalone and hybrid models, such as MLP (83%), LSTM (85%), Bi-LSTM (82%), EDBi-LSTM (80%), AWNN (78%), CEEMDAN-LSTM (83%), CEEMDAN EDBi-LSTM (80%), TVF-EMD-LSTM (65%), and TVF-EMD BiLSTM (54%). Besides, the performance of TVF-EMD-AWNN remains consistent even at multi-step hourly predictions. Therefore, the proposed hybrid framework with additional benchmarking, ablation studies, and time series validation offers robustness towards practical power system operation.

title: FedFEC: Tailored Learning via Multi-Level Knowledge Standardization and Fusion to Align Knowledge in Mobile Edge Computing_supp1-3691382.pdf
authors: Zhenyu Wang
publish: N/A
abstract: As awareness of privacy protection for mobile devices increases, the application of federated learning in Mobile Edge Computing (MEC) has attracted widespread attention. Federated learning enables the cooperative training of a robust and capable global model across mobile devices without accessing private data. However, the communication overhead and model heterogeneity issues pose significant challenges for federated learning in MEC. Although federated distillation can serve as a viable approach to reduce communication overhead through knowledge distillation and facilitate knowledge transfer among heterogeneous models, the mismatch in knowledge amplitude due to differing parameter scales between local and global models can severely impact its effectiveness. To address these challenges, we propose a flexible knowledge standardization method to align the knowledge amplitude between teacher and student models. Additionally, we introduce a multi-level knowledge fusion federated distillation approach, FedFEC, designed to enhance the performance of federated distillation for MEC. FedFEC selects clients using the Wasserstein distance, aligns knowledge via a standardization function based on model size, and fuses multi-granularity knowledge for effective server-side distillation. Clients then update local personalized model based on multi-level global knowledge. Extensive experiments conducted on four datasets demonstrate that FedFEC outperforms all benchmarks across various scenarios of data heterogeneity and model architectures.

title: BLR-Krylov: A Single-GPU Iterative SpMM Framework with Communication Avoidance and Block Low-Rank Optimization
authors: Wen Li, Zhèng Zhāng, Jie Zhao, Song Zhu, Ming Hui
publish: ACM Transactions on Architecture and Code Optimization
abstract: None
